My name is Mary. I came from Russia. I did not speak English. But I wanted to learn very much.

On my first day of school in America, I sat in the baby class. The chair was small. I was twelve years old. Most children in that class were six. But I did not mind. I was so happy to be there.

My teacher was Miss Nixon. She was kind and patient. She made a special small class for us. We were all new to English. We were all beginners.

Each day, Miss Nixon pointed to pictures. "See a cat," she said. "Hear a dog bark." We repeated the words slowly. We helped each other. When one of us did not know a word, another would help.

English was very hard. One small word was the hardest of all: "the." My mouth could not make the right sound. I said it wrong again and again. Miss Nixon did not give up. She worked with me every day.

One day in class, the teacher asked a math question. She looked at me. "If Johnnie has three marbles, and Charlie has twice as many, how many marbles has Charlie?"

I raised my hand. "Teacher," I said, "I don't know vhat is tvice."

The teacher smiled. She walked to my desk. She whispered the meaning of the word to me. Then I could solve the problem. It was a small moment. But I never forgot it. She helped me when I needed help most.

My next teacher was Miss Dillingham. I loved her very much. One day, I made a mistake. I talked during morning prayer. The teacher did not know the reason. She moved me from the good row to the low row. I felt ashamed. But I kept working hard.

Miss Dillingham helped me with another hard word: "water." I said "vater." She made exercises for my mouth. We practiced together many times. The day I said "water" correctly, I felt very proud. My teacher smiled. We had done it together.

One day, Miss Dillingham asked me to write about snow. I sat at home and wrote. I was nervous. My English was still new and broken.

I wrote: "Snow is frozen moisture which comes from the clouds. Now the snow is coming down in feather-flakes." I described the bare trees. I described the white world outside.

A few weeks later, Miss Dillingham called me to her desk after school. She showed me a page from a printed journal. There were my words. My name was at the bottom: MARY ANTIN.

I stared at my name. I could not believe it. Those were my words. My own words. In a real journal. For people to read.

I thought: I can write again. I can write many things. Maybe I can write a book!

At the end of the year, I moved to a higher class. I walked home with big books under my arm. I was proud to carry them. They were a sign that I was a student now. A real student.

I had come to America with no English. Now I could read. I could write. My teachers had not given up on me. And I had not given up on myself.
